story:
They hadn’t spoken in over two years, hadn’t dated in almost three. Heidi had lived on crumbs—odd texts, a late-night call, glimpses of him around town. She saw him everywhere: around a corner in a shop aisle, through her car window as he walked his street, in her favorite coffee shop—the one she’d had to stop visiting because she’d shown it to him and he’d made it his. She had picked his cologne, too. He still wore it. That had to mean something.

When his message came—can I come over, please, if it’s not too much? I need to stop for a moment—she almost couldn’t breathe. She cracked the door open. His hand brushed hers. Fire up her arm, into her throat. He sat on her sofa like he always used to: shoes off, hair mussed, glasses on the table, fingers laced, shoulders hunched. She offered water, a blanket, his favorite movie and snacks. He only shook his head, eyes shut tight, jaw locked.

“How are you?” she whispered.

“I don’t know,” he rasped. “I don’t know what to think.”

She stayed close but not touching, then couldn’t help herself—fingers hovering, barely resting on his thigh. He didn’t pull away. His pulse seemed to thrum under her palm. Thump, thump, thump.

He lifted his face; his mouth trembled. Don’t stare at his lips, Heidi. She stared anyway.

“I woke up and she wasn’t there,” he said. “Blocked me. Then I found a note.” He swallowed. “She left me. Said she met someone richer. Moving to America.” A tear tracked his cheek. He had been planning a proposal.

Heidi pressed her hand more firmly, fury flaring under the ache. Married? To her? She stroked his hair, felt the shampoo, the deodorant, the sweat he’d tried to hide. “I missed you,” slipped out. “More than anything.”

He blinked up. “You did?”

She nodded. “Can I be honest?” Almost honest. “She was never right for you. She didn’t see you. The night she left, the night she went to Smith’s with her friends—”

He flinched. Space opened between them.

“How did you know it was Smith’s?” he asked quietly. “I didn’t tell you that.”

Her tongue felt thick. “Didn’t you?”

“No.” His eyes sharpened. “You told me you’d leave her alone. That she would be safe.” Each word pushed him farther away, her hand falling cold from his leg. “Where is she, Heidi? What have you done?”

Panic blistered. Heat, then dread. She reached for him; he dodged. He grabbed her shoulders, shook her. “Heidi!”

Snap. Squelch. Crack. Thud.

Silence.

She could breathe again—but it was a wrong kind of quiet, a quiet made of harm. Because she had watched. Because she had lingered in doorways and windows and restaurants. Because she had wanted him so badly that she’d told herself it was love, not control. She had “made sure it all went this way,” arranged pieces she had no right to touch, convincing herself it was for him when it was only for herself.

He had come to stop for a moment, to grieve, maybe to be held by something familiar. He found the truth instead. The coffee shop he’d “stolen,” the cologne she’d chosen, the movie, the snacks waiting on the shelf—every comfort turned into a trap when the mask slipped. Her gentle words—You are amazing, she didn’t deserve you—hid the scrape of need underneath. Her “I missed you” was real; so was the obsession that had hollowed her out. In the end, there was no reunion, no healing. Only the echo of a heartbeat that had sped for the wrong reasons, and a silence that should never have fallen.
